title: Bonuses for Cost-Cutters Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
plain_summary: This bill gives government workers cash rewards for finding ways their agencies waste money. Workers can get up to twenty thousand dollars if they spot wasteful spending that their agency agrees is not needed. The agency's money expert has to check that it really is waste. Then the agency must tell the President about the wasted money so it can be cut from the budget. This program already existed but now includes more types of waste and gives bigger rewards than before.
last_action_text: Committee Consideration and Mark-up Session Held
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Bonuses for Cost-Cutters Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2  Cost Savings Enhancements
sections.text: This section changes the rules about giving money awards to government workers who find ways to save money. First, it adds a new definition for wasteful expenses. Wasteful expenses are money set aside for salaries, operations, maintenance, or similar costs that an employee says is wasteful and that the Chief Financial Officer (the person in charge of an agency's money) agrees is not needed for its intended purpose. The section also gives agency heads the power to pay cash awards to employees who identify wasteful expenses to the Chief Financial Officer that result in cost savings for the agency. The maximum award amount increases from ten thousand dollars to twenty thousand dollars. When the Chief Financial Officer decides that wasteful expenses identified by an employee meet the requirements, the agency head must tell the President so the President can propose cutting that spending through a special budget process. If an agency does not have a Chief Financial Officer, the agency head must choose someone to make these decisions about wasteful expenses. Each agency must report information about wasteful expenses that employees find, including descriptions of each case that has merit and the number and amount of cash awards given. Some people cannot receive these awards, including workers in Inspector General offices and people who are not eligible under other award rules. The Office of Personnel Management must make sure each agency follows these rules and report to Congress each year about whether agencies are following them. The Comptroller General (the head of the government's audit office) must report to Congress about how this program is working three years after this law starts and then every three years for six more years, including suggestions for changes.
